questions.question: Explain the CSMA/CD protocol and why it is used in Ethernet.
keyPoints: CSMA/CD stands for Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection
Devices listen to check if medium is idle before transmitting
If two devices transmit simultaneously, collision is detected
Jam signal is sent to notify all devices
Random backoff time is waited before retransmission
Essential for shared media (bus topology/hub) to avoid data collisions

questions.question: Draw and explain the Ethernet frame structure.
keyPoints: Preamble (7 bytes) - clock synchronization
SFD (1 byte) - Start Frame Delimiter (10101011)
Destination MAC (6 bytes) - receiver's physical address
Source MAC (6 bytes) - sender's physical address
Type/Length (2 bytes) - protocol type or data length
Data (46-1500 bytes) - payload
FCS (4 bytes) - Frame Check Sequence for error detection

questions.question: What is the difference between a hub and a switch in Ethernet? Give suitable diagram.
keyPoints: Hub operates at Physical Layer (Layer 1) - repeats signals to ALL ports
Switch operates at Data Link Layer (Layer 2) - learns MAC addresses and forwards selectively
Hub creates single collision domain; each switch port is separate collision domain
Hub bandwidth shared; switch provides dedicated bandwidth per port
Switch is more efficient and secure

questions.question: Explain the concept of auto-negotiation in Ethernet networks.
keyPoints: Auto-negotiation defined in IEEE 802.3ab for twisted pair
Allows devices to automatically determine highest common speed (10/100/1000 Mbps)
Also determines half/full duplex mode
Process uses Fast Link Pulses (FLP) for communication
If auto-negotiation fails, defaults to 10 Mbps half-duplex

questions.question: Calculate the minimum and maximum size of an Ethernet frame with explanation.
keyPoints: Minimum frame = 64 bytes: 6+6+2+46+4 = 64 bytes
Maximum frame = 1518 bytes: 6+6+2+1500+4 = 1518 bytes
Minimum data = 46 bytes (if less, padding is added)
Maximum data = 1500 bytes (MTU)
Minimum ensures collision detection at max cable length
Maximum prevents one station from dominating the medium

questions.question: What are VLANs? Explain how VLAN tagging works using 802.1Q.
keyPoints: VLAN (Virtual Local Area Network) creates logical network segments
802.1Q adds 4-byte tag in Ethernet frame between Source MAC and Type/Length
Tag contains: TPID (2B) = 0x8100 + TCI (2B) = Priority (3b) + DEI (1b) + VLAN ID (12b)
VLAN ID identifies the virtual network (1-4094)
Enables broadcast domain separation without physical cabling changes
Trunk ports carry traffic for multiple VLANs

questions.question: Explain the evolution of Ethernet from 10 Mbps to 400 Gbps. Discuss the different standards, their media types, and typical applications.
keyPoints: 10BASE5 (1980s) - Thick coax, 500m segment, vampire taps
10BASE2 - Thin coax, 185m segment, BNC connectors
10BASE-T (1990s) - Twisted pair, star topology, 100m max
100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet, 1995) - 100 Mbps, Cat5, auto-negotiation
1000BASE-T (Gigabit Ethernet, 1999) - 1 Gbps, Cat5e/6
10GBASE-T (2003) - 10 Gbps, Cat6a/7, data centers
40GBASE/100GBASE (2010) - Backbone, fiber optics
400GBASE (2017+) - High-speed data centers, dark fiber

questions.question: Explain full-duplex vs half-duplex Ethernet. Why is CSMA/CD not required in full-duplex mode? Discuss the advantages of switches over hubs in modern networks.
keyPoints: Half-duplex: one-way communication at a time, uses CSMA/CD, shared bandwidth
Full-duplex: simultaneous send/receive on separate wire pairs, no collisions
CSMA/CD not needed in full-duplex because collision domain eliminated
Advantages of switches over hubs:
- Each port is separate collision domain
- Learning table stores MAC addresses
- Frames forwarded only to destination port (reduces traffic)
- Provides dedicated bandwidth per port
- Better security (frames not broadcast to all ports)
- Supports full-duplex operation

questions.question: A company has 100 computers on a single hub-based Ethernet network. The network is experiencing slow performance and frequent collisions. Explain: (a) Why collisions occur and how CSMA/CD handles them. (b) How replacing the hub with a switch would solve these problems.
keyPoints: (a) Collisions occur when two stations transmit simultaneously on shared medium
CSMA/CD: Listen before transmit (carrier sense), detect collision while transmitting
When collision detected: stop transmitting, send jam signal, wait random backoff
Backoff increases exponentially after each collision attempt
(b) Switch solution:
- Each port is separate collision domain (no collisions between ports)
- Store-and-forward switching learns MAC addresses
- Forward frames only to destination port (not broadcast)
- Dedicated bandwidth per port (no sharing)
- Supports full-duplex (eliminates CSMA/CD entirely)
- Reduces broadcast traffic and improves security

questions.question: Explain the purpose and working of MAC addresses in Ethernet. How does a switch learn and maintain its MAC address table? Discuss what happens when a frame arrives with unknown destination MAC.
keyPoints: MAC Address: 48-bit unique identifier burned into NIC (e.g., 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E)
First 24 bits = OUI (manufacturer ID), last 24 bits = device ID
Frames use MAC for Layer 2 delivery
Switch MAC Learning:
- When frame arrives, switch reads source MAC and associates with incoming port
- Entry added to MAC address table with port and timestamp
- Frame forwarded based on destination MAC
- If unknown (unknown unicast), flood to all except source port
- If broadcast (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF), flood to all except source port
- Old entries timeout (typically 5 minutes) if not refreshed
